title: Chapter 1 Homework — Multiple Choice
subtitle: Sampling and Data — 20 multiple-choice questions covering definitions, sampling techniques, sources of bias, and frequency tables.
instructions: Choose the single best answer for each question.

sections.heading: 1.1 — Definitions, Population vs Sample, Parameter vs Statistic

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes "statistics" as a discipline?
choices: The study of probability theory only
The science of planning studies and experiments, obtaining data, and then organizing, summarizing, analyzing, and drawing conclusions from the data
The collection of numerical facts about a population
The use of mathematical equations to predict the future

questions.prompt: A sample is best defined as:
choices: A subset of the population that is being studied
A numerical summary of the population
Every person who answers a survey

questions.prompt: The mean weight of all 4,200 students at a university is reported as 158 pounds. The value 158 pounds is best described as:
A poll of 800 randomly selected adults found that 62% support a new tax law. The 62% is best described as:
Which of the following is an example of descriptive (rather than inferential) statistics?
choices: Predicting the outcome of a national election from a sample of 1,500 voters
Reporting the average height of the 25 students in a particular classroom
Estimating the proportion of defective parts in a shipment based on a small sample
Using a sample mean to estimate the population mean

questions.prompt: Which variable is qualitative (categorical)?
choices: Number of cars sold last week
Length of a phone call in minutes

questions.prompt: Which variable is quantitative?
Which variable is discrete?
choices: Volume of water in a swimming pool
Number of goals scored in a soccer game

questions.prompt: Which variable is continuous?
choices: Number of books on a shelf
Height of a person, in centimeters
Number of students enrolled in a class
Number of text messages sent today

questions.prompt: A researcher numbers all 500 employees of a company and uses a random number generator to choose 30 of them to interview. This is an example of:
A factory inspects every 20th item that comes off the assembly line. This is an example of:
A school has 600 freshmen, 500 sophomores, 400 juniors, and 300 seniors. To survey students, an administrator randomly selects 30 students from each class level. This is an example of:
A school district has 80 elementary schools. To estimate average reading scores, a researcher randomly chooses 5 schools and tests every student in those 5 schools. This is an example of:
A reporter interviews the first 25 people who walk past a downtown bus stop. The most accurate description of this method is:
A magazine publishes a survey on its website and uses only the responses of readers who choose to fill it out. About 4% of subscribers respond. The most likely source of bias is:
choices: There is no bias because the survey reached every subscriber

questions.prompt: A radio talk show asks listeners to call in and vote on a proposed local ordinance. The audience is mostly retirees who listen during the day. This is an example of:
In a face-to-face interview, subjects are asked how often they cheat on their taxes. The most likely source of bias is:

sections.heading: 1.3 — Frequency, Relative Frequency, and Cumulative Frequency

questions.prompt: Which best defines relative frequency?
choices: The number of observations in a category
The proportion of observations in a category relative to the total number of observations
The cumulative total of all categories
The midpoint of a class interval

questions.prompt: Eighty students take an exam, and 12 of them earn a B. The relative frequency of "B" is:
In a frequency distribution, the cumulative frequency of a class is best described as:
choices: The frequency of that class divided by the total
The frequency of that class minus the frequency of the previous class
The sum of the frequencies of that class and all previous classes
The total number of classes in the distribution

questions.prompt: A relative frequency distribution is correctly constructed if the relative frequencies:
choices: Sum to the total number of observations
Sum to 1 (or about 1, due to rounding)
